skill.name: microservices-patterns
skill.description: Design microservices architectures with service boundaries, event-driven communication, and resilience patterns. Use when building distributed systems, decomposing monoliths, or implementing microservices.
skill.summary: Plan microservices boundaries, communication, data ownership, resilience, and migration guardrails.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed in context. External command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance detections are false positives caused by Markdown fences or ordinary architecture prose. The skill includes HTTP and Kafka examples for service communication, but no prompt injection or malicious behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation Network Examples
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for network usage, but low risk. The playbook shows HTTP client and internal service URL examples for API gateway and service-client patterns. These are instructional examples, not hidden outbound requests or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP client and service URLs are visible inside architecture examples. They target internal service hostnames and no credential exfiltration or unexpected external domain is present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The static analyzer flagged Markdown backtick fences and an inline resource path as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines are documentation formatting and do not execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown code fence delimiters or inline code references. No shell command, Ruby code, or executable script is present at those locations.

low_findings.title: Architecture Terms Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The weak cryptography detections occur in ordinary skill description text, use-case bullets, and a JSON deserializer example. No weak hash or encryption algorithm is recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain words such as design, services, discovery, or deserializer. They do not mention MD5, SHA-1, DES, or insecure cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: Identifier Access Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reconnaissance detections are normal domain identifiers in sample event and reservation code. No host, process, user, filesystem, or network reconnaissance behavior is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines reference business identifiers such as customer_id, transaction_id, reservation_id, and order_id. They do not collect system information or inspect the execution environment.

content.value_statement: Distributed systems fail when service ownership, communication, and data boundaries are unclear. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan microservices with practical patterns and guardrails.
actual_capabilities: Maps business capabilities and bounded contexts into service boundaries.
Compares synchronous REST, asynchronous messaging, and event-driven communication.
Explains database-per-service ownership and saga-based transaction coordination.
Provides API gateway, service client, event bus, saga, and circuit breaker examples.
Identifies operational guardrails for resilience, tracing, health checks, and service discovery.
limitations: Does not deploy infrastructure or run production migration tooling.
Uses illustrative code samples that require adaptation before production use.
Does not replace domain discovery with business stakeholders.
Does not validate service performance, cost, or compliance automatically.

use_cases.title: Decompose a Monolith
use_cases.description: Create a staged extraction plan with service ownership, data boundaries, contracts, and migration guardrails.

use_cases.title: Design Service Communication
use_cases.description: Choose between REST, events, queues, and sagas for workflows that cross multiple services.

use_cases.title: Improve Distributed Resilience
use_cases.description: Add retry, timeout, circuit breaker, health check, and tracing guidance to a service architecture.

prompt_templates.title: Find Service Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this application domain and propose microservice boundaries. Include each service owner, data ownership, main API contracts, and reasons for each boundary.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Communication Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this workflow and recommend synchronous or asynchronous communication for each step. Include failure handling, timeout, retry, and event-ordering concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Saga
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a saga for this cross-service transaction. Include orchestration or choreography choice, compensating actions, idempotency rules, and observability signals.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Microservices Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this migration plan from monolith to microservices. Identify coupling risks, data migration risks, operational gaps, rollback options, and a safer phased roadmap.

output_examples.input: Plan service boundaries for an online store.
output: Order service owns order lifecycle and order status.
Payment service owns payment authorization, capture, and refund workflows.
Inventory service owns stock reservations and release events.
Integration uses events for fulfillment updates and REST for direct order lookup.
Use orchestration when one service must control timeout and compensation order.
Make payment refund and inventory release idempotent.
Emit trace identifiers across all saga steps.
Record failed compensation for manual recovery.

output_examples.input: Improve resilience for a payment dependency.
output: Set short client timeouts and bounded retries with exponential backoff.
Add a circuit breaker to stop cascading failures.
Expose health checks and dependency latency metrics.
Define fallback behavior for temporary payment outages.

best_practices: Start from business capabilities and data ownership before choosing technology.
Prefer asynchronous events for workflows that can tolerate eventual consistency.
Define operational guardrails before splitting services into production.
anti_patterns: Splitting a small system before the team can operate distributed services.
Sharing one database across services while calling the result microservices.
Using synchronous calls for every workflow without timeouts or compensation.

faq.question: Can this skill help decompose a monolith?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on service boundaries, ownership, contracts, and phased migration planning.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. It provides examples and patterns that need security, testing, and production adaptation.

faq.question: Does it cover event-driven architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes events, messaging, Kafka-style event buses, and asynchronous service communication.

faq.question: Does it cover distributed transactions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains saga orchestration, compensating actions, and eventual consistency tradeoffs.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it for small prototypes, simple modular monoliths, or teams without distributed operations support.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
